title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot and Inspector Spence discuss the possibility that Lily Gopel is either Evelyn Carpenter or Sheila Randall. Poirot notes that both women have motives to hide their identities. While Mrs. Carpenter appeared innocent during questioning, Mrs. Randall was notably tense, though Poirot recalls her previous claim of receiving an anonymous letter at the Long Grass Inn, which he doubts is authentic. Spence expresses concern regarding Guy Carpenter, a prominent local figure and parliamentary candidate, whose reputation would be ruined if he were found guilty or acting as an accessory.

Poirot shifts the focus to the motivation behind keeping photographs, proposing three reasons: vanity, nostalgia, or a desire for revenge. He argues that a photo of Lily Garfield—described as unattractive—would not be kept for vanity by beautiful women like Evelyn Carpenter or Sheila Randall, nor for nostalgia, as Lily was unloved. He concludes that since the late Mrs. Upward recognized one of the photos but intentionally misled Poirot by pointing to the wrong one, she likely intended to protect the person's secret or handle the matter privately to investigate their connection to Mrs. McGinty’s death. This leads Spence to suspect Deirdre Henderson and her mother.

In a separate scene, Mrs. Weatherby returns home from the post office and collapses on her sofa. She accuses her maid, Maud Williams, of spying by rummaging through her drawers. After demanding a restorative drink of milk, egg, and brandy, Mrs. Weatherby is joined by Deirdre, who arrives with her Siamese dog. Deirdre notes that Mrs. Weatherby seems angry.
